title: Let Election Funds Gather to Protect Our Principles!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: the opening salutation omitted by OCR has been restored, the 1967 fundraising anecdote and donation instructions are retained, and clear OCR character and word-break errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Tamil Nadu's general election will come in April or May 2016. A dawn of deliverance approaches after five years of suffering. The people are ready to send the ruling party away, but we must not become complacent and think, “If not them, then naturally us.”

The ruling party strongly believes the opposition lacks unity and has no alliance. At the same time, during five years in office it has accumulated thousands of crores through every possible route. It dreams of winning the 2016 Assembly election as easily as it won the parliamentary election and Srirangam, where it scattered Rs 2,000 per vote.

With its Assembly majority, the AIADMK acts recklessly and respects no one. The moment opposition members rise, they are ordered to sit. If they persist, they are expelled. It approaches the election believing that money gathered through the back door, official power and police support are enough for victory.

We do not possess money to match it vote for vote or meet its extravagant expenditure. But should we not gather at least enough for essential and unavoidable costs? When the ruling party approaches in silk, strutting as though wearing an entire jewellery shop, should we not come at least in a simple cotton sari, wearing glass bangles if we have no gold?

This letter is my appeal to you. You are my life-like Udanpirappu who has given generously whenever I asked and never refused. If I do not ask you, whom else can I ask? That is why I write this reminder.

In the opposing camp, funds lie piled like a mountain. “Funds” is not their true name. Some gave from fear of their “fate”; others gave in return for benefits received. Those money-bullets wait to be fired at us. I have no doubt that our movement's ranks have hearts strong enough to make every bullet ricochet and fall. We therefore have no fear.

Collecting election funds is not new to us. For the 1967 election, I obeyed Perarignar Anna's command and travelled through countless villages and towns asking: “Want the flag hoisted? Give Rs 500. A small reception? Rs 1,000. Want me to enter your home for tea or coffee? Rs 5,000 to the election fund. A special meeting? Are you ready to give Rs 1 lakh?”

When I presented Rs 11 lakh to Anna at the Virugambakkam election conference, what joy appeared on his face! Announcing candidates in that joy, he asked who the Saidapet candidate was and declared amid applause: “Eleven lakh!” Can that be forgotten?

Eleven lakh was then an enormous amount, enough to send Anna, ordinarily measured in expressing both joy and sorrow, to the very limit of delight. For today's ruling party, Rs 11 lakh is trivial, because it will spend crores upon crores. How much election funding will you and Udanpirappus like you contribute to confront those crores?

Exactly one year remains, only twelve months. We have vast democratic work to complete, and raising the election fund is one part. I know that thousands of DMK cadres stand ready to perform any movement task promptly and without a frown.

The party has been divided into sixty-five districts. I have no doubt that experienced senior commanders and newly appointed young district secretaries will treat fundraising as a competition. Union secretaries should compete intensely and prepare to hear which union collected the most. Town secretaries have a fine opportunity to compete with them. Village, town, city and metropolis must begin tomorrow to prove that none is inferior to another.
Movement pioneers, frontline leaders, headquarters secretaries, wing secretaries and election-work secretaries must join district secretaries and devote substantial time to gathering a large fund.

Donation receipts will be formally printed at headquarters and distributed through district secretaries. Do not relax and say, “At last, we hold organisational office.” Among your countless tasks, give first place to raising the election fund. Local comrades must unite, visit every village and climb the steps of every home. Explain the suffering people endure under this Government and ask them to contribute what they can to remove it.

At the Mupperum Vizha, Principal Secretary younger brother Duraimurugan said of our Treasurer, “If the mother leaps eight feet, the calf will leap sixteen.” Treasurer younger brother Thalapathi Stalin replied, “Why sixteen? I am ready to leap thirty-two.” Here is a good opportunity to prove it. He himself reminded me to write this fundraising letter. I trust he will succeed, because the movement's commanders and cadres of his generation who support him in every party task will offer the same great help in raising this fund.

Udanpirappē, we are ordinary people, common people. While in opposition, these common people conduct many struggles, our commanders repeatedly enter prison, climb the long courthouse steps in dreadful false cases, bear pain and trial, and build and protect the movement.

Every event attended by me, the General Secretary or Treasurer must now include an election-fund contribution. Those coming to a movement event or meeting us in person should bring Rs 100 or Rs 200 for the election fund instead of a garland or handloom shawl. Place it in an envelope and clearly write your address and the amount upon it. Only then can the newspapers announce that your contribution reached headquarters. Seeing your name in the accounts will bring you joy.
You will feel that you stand beside the DMK, the inextinguishable lamp of Tamil society, and that your contribution will help send home a dictatorial Government hostile to democracy.

Garlanding and shawling people on a stage takes half an hour. Each evening before leaving home for Anna Arivalayam, I spend half an hour downstairs meeting everyone who has come, naming children and greeting birthdays and weddings. Those who come to meet me must now come with an election contribution.

A garland leaves with the stage. A handloom shawl remains in my hands only briefly. Give that money instead to the election fund. It will reach headquarters and become your share in facing the coming election. That will give you greater joy.

Udanpirappē, I have asked many times, and you have given again and again. That is true. You are the Udanpirappu who never said there was nothing, but gave with both hands whenever I asked. I ask you again.
I am not accustomed to asking the rich or those who possess many crores. I ask you repeatedly, and you keep giving. I trust that you will give now too.

Must I write still more fully to ask for election funds? Is one hint not enough for my loving Udanpirappu? If told to cut, you return with it bundled and tied. Let a great fund gather to protect our mountain-like principles. That shall be our election fund.
With affection,
M.K.
12 March 2015
